column1: Comparative Table of Aladdin, Genie, and Hidden Prince Stories Across Cultures

column4: Key Details
column6: Secondary Sources

column3: Sumerian tales of Bilulu and Inanna
column4: Early references to female bottle-spirits and demonic entities that could be trapped in containers. Precursors to later jinn/genie concepts.
column6: Foster, B.R. (2005). "Before the Muses: An Anthology of Akkadian Literature"

column4: Biblical and post-biblical traditions describe Solomon commanding djinn/spirits who built his temple and performed various tasks. Early example of human commanding supernatural entities.
column5: Fragments in Dead Sea Scrolls; later Talmudic stories
column6: Lassner, J. (1993). "Demonizing the Queen of Sheba: Boundaries of Gender and Culture in Postbiblical Judaism and Medieval Islam"

column4: Ancient references to a sacred lamp in the temple of Berytus (Beirut) that was believed to house a spirit or deity.
column5: Fragmentary references in Sanchuniathon's texts as preserved by Philo of Byblos
column6: Baumgarten, A.I. (1981). "The Phoenician History of Philo of Byblos"

column4: Early narrative involving a magic ring with power. Polycrates throws his ring into the sea as a sacrifice, but it returns to him in a fish.
column6: Nagy, G. (2013). "The Ancient Greek Hero in 24 Hours"

column4: Features a hidden supernatural husband, forbidden sight, and trials to regain love. Contains elements of the "hidden identity" motif common in prince tales.
column6: Anderson, G. (2000). "Fairytale in the Ancient World"

column4: Pseudepigraphical text describing Solomon's control over demons using a magical ring, forcing them to help build the temple. Influential on later Jewish, Christian, and Islamic demonology.
column6: Johnston, S.I. (2002). "The Testament of Solomon from Late Antiquity to the Renaissance"

column4: Legends of a black lamp in Constantinople that contained an oracle spirit. Said to predict the city's fate.
column6: Magdalino, P. (2006). "Occult Science and Imperial Power in Byzantine History and Historiography"

column4: Early tale featuring a protagonist who finds magical objects and encounters jinns. Contains themes later seen in Aladdin: magical beings bound to objects and hidden treasures.
column5: Early manuscripts of "The Thousand and One Nights"
column6: Irwin, R. (1994). "The Arabian Nights: A Companion"; Haddawy, H. (2010). "The Arabian Nights"

column3: "The Story of Judar and His Brothers"
column4: A poor fisherman finds a magic ring and meets a Moorish magician. Features themes of poverty to wealth, magical objects, and deception that parallel Aladdin.
column5: Early versions of "The Thousand and One Nights"
column6: Marzolph, U. (2006). "The Arabian Nights Encyclopedia"

column4: Classic tale of a fisherman who finds a copper vessel containing an imprisoned jinni, who had been trapped by King Solomon. One of the earliest detailed stories of a spirit trapped in an object.
column5: Early manuscripts of "The Thousand and One Nights"
column6: El-Shamy, H. (2004). "Types of the Folktale in the Arab World"

column4: Story of a princess who sets riddles for her suitors. Contains elements of disguised princes and trials to win a royal bride.
column6: Javadi, H. (2005). "Persian Literary Influence on English Literature"

column4: Tale featuring a magic lamp with inscriptions that summon and command supernatural beings. Contains early lamp-spirit motifs.
column6: Irwin, R. (1994). "The Arabian Nights: A Companion"

column4: Features a mysterious child found in bamboo who is revealed to be of celestial origin. Contains themes of hidden nobility and supernatural origins.
column6: Keene, D. (1999). "Seeds in the Heart: Japanese Literature from Earliest Times to the Late Sixteenth Century"

column4: Marie de France's lay about a noble girl abandoned at birth, raised as a commoner, who eventually marries a nobleman. Early European hidden nobility tale.
column6: Kinoshita, S. & McCracken, P. (2012). "Marie de France: A Critical Companion"

column4: Story of a boy who accidentally gains magical knowledge and poetic ability, eventually serving at court. Contains themes of transformation and hidden wisdom/potential.
column5: Medieval Welsh texts, including "The Book of Taliesin"
column6: Ford, P.K. (1992). "Ystoria Taliesin"

column4: Tale of a mechanical flying horse and a prince who wins a princess. Contains elements of both hidden prince narratives and magical objects (though not genies specifically).
column6: Marzolph, U. & van Leeuwen, R. (2004). "The Arabian Nights Encyclopedia"

column4: Contains the tale of a foreign prince who disguises himself to court a princess. Features disguise, revelation of true identity, and royal marriage.
column6: Meisami, J.S. (1995). "The Haft Paykar: A Medieval Persian Romance"

column4: Though not directly an Aladdin variant, shares motifs of hidden treasures, magic words for access, and a poor protagonist becoming wealthy.
column5: Later additions to "The Thousand and One Nights"
column6: Mahdi, M. (1995). "The Thousand and One Nights"

column4: Scholars suggest early versions of the Aladdin narrative circulated in oral tradition, featuring a young man who acquires magical objects and rises from poverty.
column6: Zotenberg, H. (1888). "Histoire d'Alâ al-Dîn ou la Lampe Merveilleuse"

column4: A tale about a prince who acquires a ring with a genie that grants wishes and aids in his adventures. Contains direct parallels to Aladdin's ring.
column6: Hanaway, W.L. (1970). "Persian Popular Romances Before the Safavid Period"

column4: A prince disguised in an ugly golden shell skin who is chosen by a princess despite his appearance. Contains hidden identity, magical transformation, and recognition of true worth.
column5: Thai folk tradition, later documented in "Sepha Sang Thong"
column6: Tossa, W. (2008). "Thai Tales: Folktales of Thailand"

column4: Extended epic featuring disguised princes, magical objects, supernatural beings (divs), and heroic quests. Influenced Persian and Arabic tale traditions.
column6: Pritchett, F.W. (1991). "The Romance Tradition in Urdu: Adventures from the Dastan of Amir Hamzah"

column4: Moroccan and Egyptian folk traditions about fishermen discovering sealed jars containing jinn imprisoned by King Solomon.
column5: Oral traditions documented by European travelers
column6: Doutte, E. (1909). "Magie et Religion dans l'Afrique du Nord"

column4: Early version by Straparola featuring a poor miller's son who becomes a prince through his cat's cunning. Contains themes of hidden potential and social elevation.
column6: Bottigheimer, R. (2002). "Fairy Godfather: Straparola, Venice, and the Fairy Tale Tradition"

column4: Originally "Sun, Moon, and Talia" by Basile, later adapted by Perrault. Features a prince discovering a hidden princess and breaking a spell.
column5: Basile's "Pentamerone" (1634-1636); Perrault's "Histoires ou contes du temps passé" (1697)
column6: Zipes, J. (2006). "Why Fairy Tales Stick: The Evolution and Relevance of a Genre"

column4: Though featuring a female protagonist, contains core motifs of hidden nobility, recognition, and rightful restoration to royal status.
column6: Warner, M. (1994). "From the Beast to the Blonde: On Fairy Tales and Their Tellers"

column3: "The Story of Aladdin and the Magical Lamp"
column4: Added to "The Thousand and One Nights" by Antoine Galland, who claimed to have heard it from Syrian storyteller Hanna Diyab. Set in China but with Islamic cultural elements. Features poor boy, magical lamp, jinni, princess, and evil magician.
column6: Horta, P.L. (2017). "Marvellous Thieves: Secret Authors of the Arabian Nights"; Bottigheimer, R. (2014). "Magic Tales and Fairy Tale Magic"

column4: Story cycle featuring a hero who completes seven quests, including obtaining a magic lamp with a jinn and other magical objects.
column6: Pritchett, F.W. (2003). "The Romance Tradition in Urdu: Adventures from the Dastan of Amir Hamzah"

column4: A royal transformed into an animal who must be accepted in lowly form before revealing true identity. Reverses the hidden prince motif.
column6: Tatar, M. (2003). "The Hard Facts of the Grimms' Fairy Tales"

column4: Metaphorical tale of hidden nobility/potential, with the duckling actually being a swan raised in the wrong family.
column6: Wullschlager, J. (2000). "Hans Christian Andersen: The Life of a Storyteller"

column3: "The Tale of Prince Ivan, the Firebird, and the Grey Wolf"
column4: Features a youngest son who proves his worth through magical adventures, aided by supernatural helpers.
column6: Propp, V. (1968). "Morphology of the Folktale"

column4: Turkish variant featuring a bald boy who finds a magic lamp containing a jinn. Combines the Aladdin narrative with the Turkish trickster figure.
column6: Başgöz, İ. (1998). "Turkish Folklore and Oral Literature"

column4: Following Galland's publication, Aladdin became widely adapted in European pantomimes, folk performances, and eventually early film. Its motifs influenced numerous subsequent works.
column5: Various theatrical adaptations, chapbooks, and literary retellings
column6: Warner, M. (2011). "Stranger Magic: Charmed States and the Arabian Nights"

column4: A princess forced to exchange roles with her maid and serve as a goose girl until her true identity is revealed. Reverse of typical hidden prince narrative.
column6: Tatar, M. (2004). "The Annotated Brothers Grimm"

column4: Influential film adaptation combining elements of Aladdin, flying carpet tales, and other "Arabian Nights" stories. Features a genie in a bottle who grants three wishes.
column6: Leaman, O. (2004). "Islamic Aesthetics: An Introduction"

column4: Modern adaptation of the genie concept, featuring a female genie living in a decorative bottle who serves an astronaut master.
column6: Mittell, J. (2010). "Television and American Culture"

column4: Highly influential animated adaptation that cemented popular conceptions of genies in modern culture, including the blue color, wish limitations, and personality traits.
column6: Telotte, J.P. (2010). "Animating Space: From Mickey to WALL-E"

column4: Expanded the Disney Aladdin universe with additional genies, magical objects, and hidden identity narratives.
column6: Wells, P. (2002). "Animation and America"

column4: Modern reimagining featuring a genie character and multiple hidden identity narratives, including princes and princesses under curses or in disguise.
column6: Greenhill, P. & Rudy, J.T. (2014). "Channeling Wonder: Fairy Tales on Television"

column4: Updated version of the Disney animated film, featuring expanded mythology around genies and lamp magic.
column6: Pão, S.M. (2020). "Disney Live-Action Adaptations and Cultural Translation"
